content.text: Building a Redis-Backed Cache Layer in Go with Uber FX 🧠

content.html: Redis is a powerful tool for caching, session management, and ephemeral storage. But integrating it cleanly into a Go project — especially one using Uber FX — requires thoughtful design. In this article, we’ll walk through how to build a flexible, idiomatic Redis-backed cache layer that supports any type, avoids unnecessary abstractions, and fits naturally into a modular Go architecture.

content.alt: Building a Redis-Backed Cache Layer in Go with Uber FX 🧠

content.html: We start by organizing our cache logic under internal/storage/cache, keeping it clean and domain-agnostic:

content.code:
internal/├── storage/│   ├── cache/│   │   ├── client.go        // Redis connection setup│   │   ├──
store.go         // Cache logic (set/get/delete)│   │   └── module.go        // Uber FX integration

content.html: This sets up a Redis client using the official go-redis library.

It’s ready for production and extensible for metrics, health checks, and pub/sub. Whether you’re caching products, sessions, or search results, this design keeps your Go code clean, maintainable, and scalable.

content.alt: Building a Redis-Backed Cache Layer in Go with Uber FX 🧠
Building a Redis-Backed Cache Layer in Go with Uber FX 🧠

content.html: Designing a Redis-backed cache layer in Go doesn’t have to be complicated. By focusing on idiomatic patterns, modular structure, and Uber FX integration, we’ve built a system that’s clean, extensible, and production-ready. Whether you’re caching products, sessions, or search results, this approach gives you:

items: 🔧 A flexible Store that handles any type via JSON
🧠 Predictable key naming for domain clarity
